original_abstract: Improving the efficiency of catalytic dinitrogen ($N_2$) reduction is a central challenge in developing sustainable alternatives to the Haber–Bosch process. This study explores the production of ammonia ($NH_3$) under ambient conditions utilizing molybdenum complexes supported by N-heterocyclic carbene-based PCP-type pincer ligands, with samarium diiodide ($SmI_2$) and water serving as the reductant and proton source, respectively. Kinetic analyses reveal a concentration-dependent shift in the rate-determining step: at high catalyst concentrations, the reaction rate is governed by proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET) during N–H bond formation at the molybdenum imide intermediate, while at low concentrations, catalyst dimerization becomes rate-limiting. Toward optimizing this system, density functional theory calculations suggested that introducing electron-withdrawing groups onto the benzimidazole backbone of the pincer ligand would stabilize the LUMO of key nitride intermediates and strengthen the Mo–C(carbene) interaction. Guided by these insights, a series of substituted complexes were synthesized and evaluated. A trifluoromethyl-substituted molybdenum trichloride complex emerged as a exceptionally robust catalyst, achieving a turnover number of up to 60,000 equivalents of ammonia per molybdenum atom and a turnover frequency as high as 800 equivalents per minute. These results represent a significant advancement in molecular nitrogen fixation, offering a framework for the rational design of high-performance catalysts for next-generation ammonia synthesis.
humanized_abstract: Catalytic dinitrogen reduction remains a key focus area in the search for low energy ammonia synthesis methods. Here we report the synthesis and catalytic investigation of a range of molybdenum precursors probed by N-heterocyclic carbene-based PCP-type pincer ligands toward ambient pressure ammonia synthesis. These investigations utilized the established $SmI_2\cdot nH_2O$ reducing environment and resulting ammonia yields at varying catalyst concentrations suggested a redox-dependent transition from catalyst activation to the rate determining steps of the reaction cycle. At high catalyst concentration the rate determining step was found to be the proton coupled electron transfer step during the N–H bonding to the molybdenum imide. This is switched to catalyst dimerization at lower concentrations. Aiming to modulate the rate determining activation energy for ammonia synthesis, DFT studies pointed toward the utilization of electron withdrawing groups on the benzimidazole of the pincer ligand to facilitate a higher LUMO energy for the nitride for catalyst activation while also increasing the Mo–C(carbene) bond strength. Substituted pincer complexes along with the parent $MoCI_3\left(PCP\right)$ complex precursor were synthesized and catalytic investigations of the performance of these derivatives indicated a trifluoromethyl-group containing $MoCI_3$ complex-derived catalyst to give the highest overall performance to date, namely a TO NH3 of 60,000 and a TOF of 800.
